satisfaction.reasoning: Almost everything here works — the pacing, the humor, the non-visual sensory detail — but the broth description doesn't quite land for me. 'Deep and fatty and impossibly rich' is doing the heavy lifting but it's still kind of... standard food writing? It doesn't make me pause the way a really specific detail would.

Almost everything is working really well — the broth description, the water glass moment, the pacing, the humor. The one thing holding it back slightly is that inside the shop, the non-visual sensory detail kind of disappears. We get a lot of what it looks like — the stools, the counter, the sign — but once I'm actually inside, I'm not feeling or hearing or smelling anything. The rain covers the exterior scenes, but the interior reads a bit like a photograph.

Every preference is fully met — the sensory details are specific and physical rather than generic, the anecdotes connect directly to the next beat, the pacing moves in and out of immersion naturally, humor comes from honest self-observation, and every scene has strong non-visual sensory grounding. No dealbreakers triggered.

Almost everything here really works — the cold, the humor, the broth description. But the sensory grounding of the actual space leans heavily visual, and the one smell/sound moment feels a little vague.

Almost everything is working really well here — the sensory details are specific and alive, the humor lands honestly, and the room finally feels like somewhere you're sitting in rather than looking at. The one thing that's nagging at me is the tipping anecdote at the end — it's funny and self-aware, which I love, but it kind of floats there on its own. It doesn't feel like it's going anywhere or setting anything up.

Every preference is fully met — the sensory details are specific and non-visual, the anecdote earns its place by landing on the tipping observation, the pacing moves in and out of scene naturally, and the humor is self-aware without trying too hard.

Every preference is technically met and met well — the sensory work is genuinely good. But this is round 1 and a real reader would catch that the ending lands a little too neatly; 'I just knew I didn't want to move' is close to a stock closing sentiment and slightly undercuts the specificity of everything before it.

Every preference lands — the sensory details are genuinely unexpected, the anecdote earns its place by setting up the broth context, the pacing moves in and out of the scene naturally, the humor is dry and observational, and every scene has something you can hear or feel, not just see. No dealbreakers triggered.

Every preference is technically met — the sensory details are sharp, the anecdote earns its place, the rhythm between immersion and context works. But this is round one and the final paragraph feels like it arrives at its conclusion a little too cleanly — the insight about mole negro as a 'record' is interesting but it lands almost like a thesis statement rather than something I'm still discovering alongside the reader.

Every preference is fully met — specific unexpected sensory details, anecdotes that earn their place by connecting directly to the grinding experience, a clean immersion-then-context-then-back rhythm, humor that comes from honest self-deprecation rather than performance, and non-visual sensory texture throughout. No clichés, no listicle moves, no promotional language.
